questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Examine the relationship between the following pairs of compounds:

Pair (i): and

Pair (ii): and

Pair (iii): and
options: Pairs (i) and (ii) represent identical compounds, while pair (iii) represents isomers.
Pair (i) represents identical compounds, while pairs (ii) and (iii) represent isomers.
Pairs (i), (ii), and (iii) all represent identical compounds.
questions.solution:
Let us analyze each pair of compounds:

Pair (i): Both structures have two chlorine atoms attached to adjacent carbon atoms of a benzene ring. They both represent 1,2-dichlorobenzene (ortho-dichlorobenzene). The different placement of the double bonds merely represents two Kekulé resonance structures of the same molecule. Therefore, they are identical compounds.

Pair (ii): In both structures, the two hydroxyl ($\text{-OH}$) groups are attached to carbon atoms separated by one carbon atom in the benzene ring. Both represent 1,3-dihydroxybenzene (meta-dihydroxybenzene or resorcinol). The second structure is simply a rotated version of the first. Therefore, they are identical compounds.

Pair (iii): The first structure has two carboxyl ($\text{-COOH}$) groups on opposite carbon atoms of the benzene ring, which is 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (terephthalic acid). The second structure has the two carboxyl groups separated by one carbon atom, which is 1,3-benzenedicarboxylic acid (isophthalic acid). Since they have the same molecular formula but different positions of the functional groups, they are positional isomers.

Conclusion: Pairs (i) and (ii) represent identical compounds, while pair (iii) represents isomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is an isomer of ethanol?
questions.solution:
The molecular formula of ethanol ($CH_3CH_2OH$) is $C_2H_6O$.

Isomers are compounds with the same molecular formula but different structural formulas.

The molecular formulas of the given options are:
Acetone: $C_3H_6O$
Methanol: $CH_4O$
Dimethyl ether: $C_2H_6O$
Diethyl ether: $C_4H_{10}O$

Dimethyl ether ($CH_3OCH_3$) has the same molecular formula as ethanol ($C_2H_6O$) but a different functional group (ether instead of alcohol). Thus, it is a functional isomer of ethanol.

Answer: dimethyl ether

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The compounds $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ exhibit which of the following types of isomerism?
questions.solution:
The molecular formula of both compounds is $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{9}\mathrm{N}$.

$(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{N}$ is a tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) amine.

$\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ is a primary ($1^{\circ}$) amine.

Primary, secondary, and tertiary amines are considered different functional groups. Therefore, compounds having the same molecular formula but differing in the degree of the amine group exhibit functional isomerism.

Answer: Functional isomerism

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following structures represents ( $\mathrm{R}, \mathrm{Z}$ )-7-methoxy-2,7-dimethyl-4-propylnona-1,4-diene?
questions.solution:
To determine the correct structure, we evaluate the stereochemistry at the C4=C5 double bond and the C7 chiral center.

Priorities for the E/Z configuration at C4=C5:
At C4: The $-CH_2-C(CH_3)=CH_2$ group (C attached to C, C, C) has higher priority than the $-CH_2CH_2CH_3$ group (C attached to C, H, H).
At C5: The $-CH_2-C(OCH_3)(CH_3)-CH_2CH_3$ group has higher priority than the $-H$ atom.
In structures (A) and (B), both higher priority groups are on the same side of the double bond, giving the (Z) configuration. Structures (C) and (D) have the (E) configuration.

Priorities for the R/S configuration at C7:
1. $-OCH_3$ (highest atomic number of attached atom)
2. $-CH_2-CH=C...$ (C attached to C, C, H)
3. $-CH_2CH_3$ (C attached to C, H, H)
4. $-CH_3$ (C attached to H, H, H)

In structure (B), the lowest priority group ($-CH_3$) is on a dashed bond (pointing away). The sequence from priority 1 to 2 to 3 ($-OCH_3 \rightarrow -CH_2-CH=C... \rightarrow -CH_2CH_3$) traces a clockwise direction. This corresponds to the (R) configuration.

In structure (A), the $-CH_3$ group is on a wedged bond (pointing towards), which gives the (S) configuration.

Therefore, structure (B) represents (R, Z)-7-methoxy-2,7-dimethyl-4-propylnona-1,4-diene.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the total number of structural isomers possible for the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{4} \mathrm{H}_{11} \mathrm{N}$?
questions.solution:
The degree of unsaturation for the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{4}\mathrm{H}_{11}\mathrm{N}$ is $4 + 1 - \dfrac{11}{2} + \dfrac{1}{2} = 0$. Thus, the compound is a saturated acyclic amine.

The possible structural isomers can be classified into primary ($1^{\circ}$), secondary ($2^{\circ}$), and tertiary ($3^{\circ}$) amines.

Primary amines ($1^{\circ}$) have the general formula $\mathrm{C}_{4}\mathrm{H}_{9}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$. Since there are four isomeric butyl groups, there are four primary amines:
1. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ (n-butylamine)
2. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{NH}_{2})\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (sec-butylamine)
3. $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}\mathrm{CHCH}_{2}\mathrm{NH}_{2}$ (isobutylamine)
4. $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{CNH}_{2}$ (tert-butylamine)

Secondary amines ($2^{\circ}$) have the general formula $\mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{NH}-\mathrm{R}_{2}$. The alkyl groups can be methyl and propyl, or two ethyl groups. This gives three secondary amines:
5. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NHCH}_{3}$ (methyl n-propylamine)
6. $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}\mathrm{CHNHCH}_{3}$ (methyl isopropylamine)
7. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{NHCH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (diethylamine)

Tertiary amines ($3^{\circ}$) have the general formula $\mathrm{R}_{1}-\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{R}_{2})-\mathrm{R}_{3}$. The only possible combination of alkyl groups with a total of four carbon atoms is two methyl groups and one ethyl group:
8. $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}\mathrm{NCH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (ethyldimethylamine)

Total number of structural isomers = $4 + 3 + 1 = 8$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many stereocenters are present in the given structure of latomoxef?
questions.solution:
To determine the number of stereocenters, we identify $sp^3$ hybridized carbon atoms bonded to four different groups.

Examining the structure of latomoxef:

1. The bridgehead carbon of the bicyclic core is bonded to an oxygen atom, a nitrogen atom, a carbon atom of the $\beta$-lactam ring, and a hydrogen atom. This is a stereocenter.

2. The adjacent carbon in the $\beta$-lactam ring is bonded to the bridgehead carbon, a carbonyl carbon, a methoxy group ($-OCH_3$), and an amide nitrogen. This is a second stereocenter.

3. In the side chain, the carbon atom attached to the amide carbonyl is bonded to a carboxyl group ($-COOH$), a p-hydroxyphenyl group, the carbonyl group, and a hydrogen atom. This is a third stereocenter.

All other $sp^3$ carbons are part of $CH_2$ or $CH_3$ groups, which have identical hydrogen atoms and are not stereocenters. The remaining carbons are $sp^2$ hybridized.

Therefore, the molecule has a total of 3 stereocenters.

Answer: 3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines possible for the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{9}\mathrm{N}$ are, respectively:
questions.solution:
The molecular formula is $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{9}\mathrm{N}$.

For primary amines ($1^{\circ}$), the general formula is $\mathrm{R-NH_2}$. The alkyl group $\mathrm{R}$ is $\mathrm{C_3H_7}$, which has two isomeric forms: n-propyl and isopropyl. Thus, there are two primary amines:
1. $\mathrm{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2}$ (Propan-1-amine)
2. $\mathrm{CH_3-CH(NH_2)-CH_3}$ (Propan-2-amine)

For secondary amines ($2^{\circ}$), the general formula is $\mathrm{R_1-NH-R_2}$. The alkyl groups must contain a total of 3 carbon atoms. The only possible combination is a methyl group and an ethyl group. Thus, there is one secondary amine:
1. $\mathrm{CH_3-NH-CH_2-CH_3}$ (N-Methylethanamine)

For tertiary amines ($3^{\circ}$), the general formula is $\mathrm{R_1-N(R_2)-R_3}$. The alkyl groups must contain a total of 3 carbon atoms. The only possible combination is three methyl groups. Thus, there is one tertiary amine:
1. $\mathrm{CH_3-N(CH_3)-CH_3}$ (N,N-Dimethylmethanamine)

The number of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines are 2, 1, and 1 respectively.

Answer: 2, 1, 1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the total number of possible structural isomers for methylanthracene?
questions.solution:
Anthracene consists of three fused benzene rings in a linear arrangement. Due to its symmetry, the carbon atoms available for substitution in anthracene can be grouped into three sets of equivalent positions:

Positions $1, 4, 5,$ and $8$ are equivalent ($\alpha$-positions).
Positions $2, 3, 6,$ and $7$ are equivalent ($\beta$-positions).
Positions $9$ and $10$ are equivalent ($\gamma$ or meso-positions).

Since there are exactly three distinct types of positions available for substitution, introducing a single methyl group will yield three different structural isomers:
$1$-methylanthracene
$2$-methylanthracene
$9$-methylanthracene

Therefore, the total number of possible structural isomers for methylanthracene is $3$.

Answer: 3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which type of isomerism is exhibited by the following pair of compounds?



and
questions.solution:
The given compounds are cyclohexyl benzenesulfonate and phenyl cyclohexanesulfonate.

Both compounds have the same molecular formula and the same polyvalent functional group, which is the sulfonate ester group ($-SO_2-O-$).

In the first compound, a phenyl group is attached to the sulfur atom and a cyclohexyl group is attached to the oxygen atom.

In the second compound, a cyclohexyl group is attached to the sulfur atom and a phenyl group is attached to the oxygen atom.

Since the two compounds differ only in the nature of the alkyl/aryl groups attached to the same polyvalent functional group, they exhibit metamerism.

Answer: Metamerism

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The isomeric relationship between 3-ethylpentane and 3-methylhexane is:
questions.solution:
The structure of 3-ethylpentane is $CH_3-CH_2-CH(CH_2CH_3)-CH_2-CH_3$. Its principal carbon chain contains 5 carbon atoms.

The structure of 3-methylhexane is $CH_3-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3$. Its principal carbon chain contains 6 carbon atoms.

Both compounds have the same molecular formula ($C_7H_{16}$) but differ in the number of carbon atoms in the principal carbon chain.

Therefore, they exhibit chain isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Identify whether the following statements regarding the isomeric relationships between the given pairs of compounds are True (T) or False (F).

(I) and are functional isomers.
(II) and are metamers.
(III) and are metamers.
(IV) and are functional isomers.
questions.solution:
Let us analyze each statement one by one:

Statement (I): The first compound is $o$-propylbenzoic acid, which contains a carboxylic acid ($-\text{COOH}$) functional group. The second compound is 2-(o-tolyl)ethyl formate, which contains an ester ($-\text{OCOH}$) functional group. Since they have the same molecular formula ($\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_2$) but different functional groups, they are functional isomers. Thus, Statement (I) is True (T).

Statement (II): The first compound is $o$-ethylanisole (methyl $o$-ethylphenyl ether), and the second compound is $m$-methylphenetole (ethyl $m$-tolyl ether). Both are ethers, but the alkyl/aryl groups attached to the polyvalent oxygen atom are different (methyl and $o$-ethylphenyl vs. ethyl and $m$-tolyl). Isomers that differ in the nature of alkyl groups attached to the same polyvalent functional group are called metamers. Thus, Statement (II) is True (T).

Statement (III): The first compound is 2-phenylethanol, which is an alcohol. The second compound is benzyl methyl ether, which is an ether. Alcohols and ethers with the same molecular formula are functional isomers, not metamers (metamerism occurs within the same functional group class). Thus, Statement (III) is False (F).

Statement (IV): The first compound is $N,N$-dimethylaniline, which is a tertiary ($3^\circ$) amine. The second compound is $N$-methylbenzylamine, which is a secondary ($2^\circ$) amine. Primary, secondary, and tertiary amines are considered functional isomers of each other because they exhibit different chemical properties. Thus, Statement (IV) is True (T).

The correct sequence is T, T, F, T.

Answer: T, T, F, T

Which of the following statements regarding their isomeric relationship is INCORRECT?
questions.solution:
Let us first identify the functional groups and structures of the given compounds:

Compound (i) is 2,4,6-trimethylcyclohexan-1-ol. It contains an alcohol ($-OH$) functional group.
Compound (ii) is 1-methoxy-3,5-dimethylcyclohexane. It contains an ether ($-O-$) functional group.
Compound (iii) is 2-(4-methylcyclohexyl)ethan-1-ol. It contains an alcohol ($-OH$) functional group.
Compound (iv) is 1-methyl-4-(methoxymethyl)cyclohexane. It contains an ether ($-O-$) functional group.

Now, let's evaluate each option:

(A) Compounds (i) and (ii) have the same molecular formula ($C_9H_{18}O$) but different functional groups (alcohol vs. ether). Thus, they are functional isomers. This statement is correct.

(B) Compounds (i) and (iii) are both alcohols. In (i), the principal carbon chain is the cyclohexane ring (6 carbons). In (iii), the principal carbon chain is the ethyl group (2 carbons) because it contains the principal functional group ($-OH$). Since their parent carbon chains are different, they are chain isomers. (D) Compound (i) is an alcohol and compound (iv) is an ether. Positional isomers must have the same functional group and differ only in its position on the carbon skeleton. Since they have different functional groups, they are functional isomers, not positional isomers. This statement is INCORRECT.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is not an isomer of the other three?
questions.solution:
To determine which compound is not an isomer of the other three, we find the molecular formula of each compound by counting the number of carbon and hydrogen atoms.

Compound (A) contains $3$ rings. It has $8$ carbon atoms. The structure consists of $4$ tertiary carbons ($CH$) and $4$ secondary carbons ($CH_2$). Its molecular formula is $C_8H_{12}$, with a degree of unsaturation of $3$.

Compound (B) contains $2$ rings. It has $8$ carbon atoms. The structure consists of $2$ tertiary carbons ($CH$) and $6$ secondary carbons ($CH_2$). Its molecular formula is $C_8H_{14}$, with a degree of unsaturation of $2$.

Compound (C) contains $2$ rings. It has $8$ carbon atoms. The structure consists of $2$ tertiary carbons ($CH$) at the bridgeheads and $6$ secondary carbons ($CH_2$). Its molecular formula is $C_8H_{14}$, with a degree of unsaturation of $2$.

Compound (D) contains $1$ ring and $1$ double bond. It has $8$ carbon atoms. The structure consists of $2$ carbons involved in a double bond ($CH$) and $6$ secondary carbons ($CH_2$). Its molecular formula is $C_8H_{14}$, with a degree of unsaturation of $2$.

Compounds (B), (C), and (D) have the same molecular formula ($C_8H_{14}$) and are isomers. Compound (A) has a different molecular formula ($C_8H_{12}$) and is not an isomer of the others.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following pairs of compounds are chain isomers?
questions.solution:
Let us analyze each option by identifying the given compounds and their relationship:

Option (A):
The first compound is 2,2-dimethylbutane (molecular formula $C_6H_{14}$).
The second compound is 2,2-dimethylpentane (molecular formula $C_7H_{16}$).
Since they have different molecular formulas, they are not isomers; they are homologs.

Option (B):
The first compound is 2,3-dimethylbutane. Its principal carbon chain has 4 carbon atoms, and its molecular formula is $C_6H_{14}$.
The second compound is 3-methylpentane. Its principal carbon chain has 5 carbon atoms, and its molecular formula is also $C_6H_{14}$.
Since they have the same molecular formula but differ in the length of their principal carbon chains, they are chain isomers.

Option (C):
The first compound is cyclohexane ($C_6H_{12}$).
The second compound is methylcyclopentane ($C_6H_{12}$).
These differ in the size of the principal ring. While sometimes considered a sub-type of chain isomerism, they are more specifically classified as nuclear isomers or ring isomers. Option (B) represents the classic and primary definition of chain isomerism.

Option (D):
The first compound is 2,3-dimethylbutane.
The second compound is also 2,3-dimethylbutane, merely drawn in a different orientation.
They are identical molecules, not isomers.

Therefore, the pair of compounds in Option (B) are chain isomers.

questions.solution:

Compound (ii) is $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{NH}_{2})-\mathrm{CHO}$ (2-aminopropanal), which contains a primary amine ($-\mathrm{NH}_{2}$) and an aldehyde ($-\mathrm{CHO}$) functional group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will NOT exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution:
To determine which compound will not exhibit tautomerism, we need to check for the presence of $\alpha$-hydrogens or allylic hydrogens that can migrate between the carbon skeleton and the heteroatom (like oxygen in a nitroso or oxime group).

Let us analyze each option:

Option (A): The given compound is an alkene with a nitroso ($-N=O$) group. The structure is $(H_3C)_3C-C(C(CH_3)_3)=C(C(CH_3)_3)-N=O$. The carbons directly attached to the $C=C$ double bond are part of tert-butyl groups ($-C(CH_3)_3$). These are quaternary carbons and possess no hydrogen atoms. Since there are no allylic hydrogens or $\alpha$-hydrogens available to migrate to the oxygen atom of the nitroso group, this compound cannot undergo tautomerism.

Option (B): The compound is an oxime, $H_3C-CH=CH-C(CH_3)=N-OH$. It can undergo vinylogous tautomerism (extended tautomerism) where an allylic hydrogen from the $\gamma$-position migrates to the oxygen, shifting the double bonds to form a nitroso compound: $CH_3-CH_2-CH=C(CH_3)-N=O \rightleftharpoons CH_3-CH=CH-C(CH_3)=N-OH$. Thus, it exhibits tautomerism.

Option (C): The compound is acetaldoxime, $H_3C-CH=N-OH$. It readily exhibits classic nitroso-oxime tautomerism by migrating a hydrogen atom: $CH_3-CH_2-N=O \rightleftharpoons CH_3-CH=N-OH$.

Option (D): The compound is $(H_3C)_3C-C(CH_3)=C(CH_3)-N=O$. It has methyl groups attached to the $C=C$ double bond. These methyl groups provide allylic hydrogens that can migrate to the oxygen atom of the nitroso group, allowing it to tautomerize into an oxime form: $CH_2=C(t-Bu)-C(CH_3)=N-OH \rightleftharpoons CH_3-C(t-Bu)=C(CH_3)-N=O$. Thus, it exhibits tautomerism.

Therefore, only the compound in option (A) lacks the necessary acidic hydrogens to exhibit tautomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds does not exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution:
For a compound to exhibit tautomerism (such as keto-enol tautomerism), it generally requires the presence of at least one acidic $\alpha$-hydrogen atom on an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon adjacent to the electron-withdrawing functional group (like a carbonyl group).

Let us analyze the given options:

Option (A): p-benzoquinone. The carbonyl groups are adjacent to $sp^2$ hybridized carbons. There are no $\alpha$-hydrogens on any $sp^3$ hybridized carbon. The hydrogens present are vinylic. If it were to undergo tautomerism, it would require the formation of a triple bond or cumulated double bonds within the six-membered ring, which is highly strained and energetically impossible. Thus, it does not exhibit tautomerism.

Option (B): 1,3,5-cyclohexanetrione. It has active $\alpha$-hydrogens on $sp^3$ hybridized carbons. It readily undergoes keto-enol tautomerism to form phloroglucinol (1,3,5-trihydroxybenzene), which is highly stable due to aromaticity.

Option (C): Benzaldoxime ($C_6H_5-CH=N-OH$). It exhibits nitroso-oxime tautomerism, existing in equilibrium with its nitroso tautomer ($C_6H_5-CH_2-N=O$).

Option (D): 1,4-cyclohexanedione. It has $\alpha$-hydrogens on $sp^3$ hybridized carbons adjacent to the carbonyl groups and can easily undergo keto-enol tautomerism.

Therefore, p-benzoquinone is the only compound that does not exhibit tautomerism.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds is not an isomer of 3-ethyl-2-methylpentane?
questions.solution:
The given compound is 3-ethyl-2-methylpentane.

The number of carbon atoms in the parent chain (pentane) is 5.

The substituents are an ethyl group (2 carbons) and a methyl group (1 carbon).

Total number of carbon atoms = $5 + 2 + 1 = 8$.

The molecular formula of 3-ethyl-2-methylpentane is $C_8H_{18}$.

Analyzing the given options:

Option (A): The longest continuous carbon chain has 6 carbon atoms. There is a methyl group at the second carbon. The IUPAC name is 2-methylhexane. The total number of carbon atoms is $6 + 1 = 7$. Its molecular formula is $C_7H_{16}$.

Option (B): The structure is a straight chain of 8 carbon atoms. The IUPAC name is octane. Its molecular formula is $C_8H_{18}$.

Option (C): The longest continuous carbon chain has 6 carbon atoms. There are two methyl groups at the second carbon. The IUPAC name is 2,2-dimethylhexane. The total number of carbon atoms is $6 + 2 = 8$. Its molecular formula is $C_8H_{18}$.

Option (D): The longest continuous carbon chain has 6 carbon atoms. There are methyl groups at the third and fourth carbons. The IUPAC name is 3,4-dimethylhexane. The total number of carbon atoms is $6 + 2 = 8$. Its molecular formula is $C_8H_{18}$.

Since the compound in option (A) has a different molecular formula ($C_7H_{16}$) from 3-ethyl-2-methylpentane ($C_8H_{18}$), it is not an isomer.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of geminal dichloride structural isomers possible for the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ is:
questions.solution:
The molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{3}\mathrm{H}_{6}\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ corresponds to a saturated acyclic compound since its degree of unsaturation is zero.

Geminal dichlorides are compounds in which both chlorine atoms are attached to the same carbon atom.

For a three-carbon chain (propane skeleton), the two chlorine atoms can be placed on either a terminal carbon or the middle carbon.

Placing both chlorine atoms on the terminal carbon gives 1,1-dichloropropane: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CHCl}_{2}$.

Placing both chlorine atoms on the middle carbon gives 2,2-dichloropropane: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CCl}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$.

Thus, there are exactly 2 geminal dichloride structural isomers possible.

Answer: 2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will not exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution:
Tautomerism in nitro compounds (nitro-aci tautomerism) is exhibited by those compounds which have at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen atom attached to the carbon bearing the $-\text{NO}_2$ group.

Let us examine the given options:

(A) $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{NO}_2)-\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_3$: The $\alpha$-carbon has one hydrogen atom.

(B) $(\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2\text{NO}_2$: The $\alpha$-carbon has two hydrogen atoms.

(C) $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}-\text{NO}_2$: The $\alpha$-carbon is attached to three methyl groups and has no hydrogen atom. Since it lacks an $\alpha$-hydrogen, it cannot exhibit tautomerism.

(D) $\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{NO}_2$: The $\alpha$-carbon has two hydrogen atoms.

Therefore, $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C}-\text{NO}_2$ will not exhibit tautomerism.

Answer: $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds can exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution:
To determine which compound can exhibit tautomerism, we need to look for the presence of at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen atom on an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon adjacent to a carbonyl group.

Let us analyze each option:

1. Compound (A): This is a diphenoquinone derivative. All the carbon atoms in the rings are $sp^2$ hybridized. There are no $sp^3$ hybridized $\alpha$-carbons, and thus no $\alpha$-hydrogens available for tautomerism.

2. Compound (C): This is anthraquinone. The carbons adjacent to the carbonyl groups are part of the aromatic benzene rings and do not possess any hydrogen atoms. Therefore, it cannot exhibit tautomerism.

3. Compound (D): This is 1,4-naphthoquinone. The fusion carbons are part of the aromatic ring and have no hydrogen atoms. The other carbons adjacent to the carbonyls are $sp^2$ hybridized. Hence, it lacks $sp^3$ $\alpha$-hydrogens and cannot tautomerize.

4. Compound (B): In this fused bicyclic system, the two fusion carbons (shared between the two rings) are $sp^3$ hybridized. Each of these fusion carbons is adjacent to a carbonyl group and possesses one $\alpha$-hydrogen atom.

These $\alpha$-hydrogens can participate in keto-enol tautomerism. When the hydrogens migrate to the carbonyl oxygens, double bonds are formed between the fusion carbons and the carbonyl carbons. This rearrangement results in the aromatization of the right ring, forming a highly stable phenol-like derivative (a dihydroxynaphthalene derivative). The strong thermodynamic driving force of aromatization makes this tautomerization highly favorable.

Thus, only compound (B) can exhibit tautomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the isomeric relationship between the two compounds shown below:
questions.solution:
Let us determine the IUPAC names or the substitution patterns of the two given compounds to identify their isomeric relationship.

Both compounds share the same parent carbon skeleton, which is a biphenyl system.

In the first compound:
On the left ring, the $-\text{COOH}$ group is at the ortho position (C2) and the $-\text{Br}$ atom is at the meta position (C3) with respect to the biphenyl linkage.
On the right ring, the $-\text{COOH}$ group is at the ortho position (C2') and the $-\text{Br}$ atom is at the meta position (C3').
Thus, the substituents are at the 2,3 and 2',3' positions. The IUPAC name is 3,3'-dibromo-[1,1'-biphenyl]-2,2'-dicarboxylic acid.

In the second compound:
On the left ring, the $-\text{COOH}$ group is at an ortho position (C2) and the $-\text{Br}$ atom is at the other ortho position (C6).
On the right ring, the $-\text{Br}$ atom is at an ortho position (C2') and the $-\text{COOH}$ group is at the other ortho position (C6').
Thus, the substituents are at the 2,6 and 2',6' positions. The IUPAC name is 6,6'-dibromo-[1,1'-biphenyl]-2,2'-dicarboxylic acid.

Since the two compounds have the same molecular formula and the same functional groups, but differ only in the positions of the substituents on the benzene rings, they are positional isomers.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds exhibits tautomerism?
questions.solution:
For a compound to exhibit tautomerism (such as keto-enol or nitro-aci tautomerism), it generally requires the presence of at least one acidic hydrogen atom. This is typically an $\alpha$-hydrogen on an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon adjacent to a strong electron-withdrawing group (like $-\mathrm{CHO}$, $-\mathrm{NO}_2$, $>\mathrm{C=O}$). In conjugated systems, a $\gamma$-hydrogen on an $sp^3$ carbon can also participate.

Let us analyze the given options:

1. Option (A) - Benzaldehyde ($\mathrm{C}_6\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{CHO}$): The carbon atom adjacent to the carbonyl group is part of the benzene ring. It is $sp^2$ hybridized and has no hydrogen atom attached to it. Since it lacks an $\alpha$-hydrogen, it does not exhibit tautomerism.

2. Option (B) - $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_2\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{NO}_2)-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CHO}$: The $\alpha$-carbon (in the $-\mathrm{CH}=$ group) is $sp^2$ hybridized, which generally does not participate in tautomerism as it would lead to a highly unstable cumulated diene system. We must also check the $\gamma$-position due to conjugation. The $\gamma$-carbon is $sp^3$ hybridized but is bonded to two methyl groups and a nitro group, meaning it has zero hydrogen atoms. The nitro group also lacks an $\alpha$-hydrogen. Therefore, this compound does not exhibit tautomerism.

3. Option (C) - $(\mathrm{CH}_3)_2\mathrm{CCl}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_2$: This compound lacks a suitable electron-withdrawing group (like a carbonyl or nitro group) to facilitate the reversible migration of a proton. Thus, it does not exhibit tautomerism.

Since none of the given compounds have the necessary structural features to exhibit tautomerism, the correct choice is "None of the above".

Answer: None of the above

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds can exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution:
Compound (1) is 1,2-cyclopentanedione. It contains $\alpha$-hydrogens adjacent to the carbonyl groups and exhibits keto-enol tautomerism, forming a stable enol.

Compound (2) is butane-2,3-dione. It possesses $\alpha$-hydrogens on the terminal methyl groups and can exhibit keto-enol tautomerism.

Compound (3) is 1,3,5-cyclooctatriene. It exhibits valence tautomerism, existing in a dynamic equilibrium with its valence isomer bicyclo[4.2.0]octa-2,4-diene.

Therefore, all the given compounds exhibit tautomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds can exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution:
Tautomerism in nitro compounds (nitro-aci tautomerism) requires the presence of at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen atom adjacent to the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group.

In $\mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{CNO}_{2}$, the $\alpha$-carbon is bonded to three alkyl groups and has no $\alpha$-hydrogen. Thus, it cannot exhibit tautomerism.

In $\mathrm{RCH}_{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, the $\alpha$-carbon has two $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms. It can exhibit nitro-aci tautomerism: $\mathrm{RCH}_{2}-\mathrm{NO}_{2} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{RCH}=\mathrm{N}(\mathrm{OH})\mathrm{O}$.

In $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CNO}$, the $\alpha$-carbon has no $\alpha$-hydrogen, so it cannot exhibit tautomerism.

In $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}$, there is no suitable electron-withdrawing multiple bond system to facilitate tautomerism.

Therefore, only $\mathrm{RCH}_{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$ can exhibit tautomerism.

Answer: $\mathrm{RCH}_{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds will exhibit tautomerism?
questions.solution:
Tautomerism is exhibited by compounds containing an active $\alpha$-hydrogen atom adjacent to a strongly electron-withdrawing group such as $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$, $-\mathrm{CHO}$, or $-\mathrm{CN}$.

In $\mathrm{RCH}_{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$, there are two $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms present on the carbon adjacent to the $-\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ group. It exhibits nitro-aci tautomerism:

$\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{N}^{+}(=\mathrm{O})\mathrm{O}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{R}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{N}^{+}(\mathrm{OH})\mathrm{O}^{-}$

In $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3} \mathrm{CNO}$ and $\mathrm{R}_{3} \mathrm{CNO}_{2}$, there are no $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms present on the carbon attached to the functional group, so they cannot exhibit tautomerism.

In $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{2} \mathrm{NH}$, there is no strongly electron-withdrawing group to facilitate tautomerism.

Answer: $\mathrm{RCH}_{2} \mathrm{NO}_{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Geometrical isomers differ from each other in the:
questions.solution:
Geometrical isomers are a type of stereoisomers. Stereoisomers have the same molecular formula and structural formula but differ in the spatial arrangement of atoms or groups of atoms in three-dimensional space.

Therefore, geometrical isomers differ from each other in the spatial arrangement of atoms.

Answer: spatial arrangement of atoms

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds can exhibit geometrical isomerism?
questions.solution:
Geometrical isomerism is exhibited by alkenes of the type $abC=Cxy$, where $a \neq b$ and $x \neq y$. Each carbon atom of the double bond must be attached to two different atoms or groups.

In $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$, the right carbon atom is attached to two identical methyl groups.

In $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{2}$, both carbon atoms are attached to identical methyl groups.

In $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCH}_{3}$ (But-2-ene), each doubly bonded carbon atom is attached to a hydrogen atom and a methyl group. Since both carbons are bonded to two different groups, it exhibits geometrical isomerism (cis and trans forms).

In $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}$, the right carbon atom is attached to two identical hydrogen atoms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds exhibits the highest percentage of enol content at equilibrium?
questions.solution:
The enol content of a compound depends on the stability of its enol form. Factors that increase enol stability include intramolecular hydrogen bonding and extended conjugation.

Compound (A) is a $\beta$-diketone (acetylacetone). Its enol form is highly stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding, which forms a stable six-membered chelate ring, and by the conjugation of the carbon-carbon double bond with the carbonyl group. The enol content is typically around $76\%$.

Compounds (B) and (D) are $\beta$-keto esters. In $\beta$-keto esters, the $+R$ effect of the alkoxy oxygen atom (cross-conjugation) competes with the enol double bond for conjugation with the carbonyl group. This reduces the stability of the enol form compared to a $\beta$-diketone. Their enol contents are generally much lower (around $8\%$ for B and $21\%$ for D).

Compound (C) is a simple ester, which has a negligible enol content (close to $0\%$).

Therefore, the $\beta$-diketone (A) exhibits the highest percentage of enol content at equilibrium.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds does NOT exhibit cis-trans isomerism?
questions.solution:
For an alkene to exhibit cis-trans (geometrical) isomerism, each carbon atom of the double bond must be attached to two different atoms or groups. If any one of the double-bonded carbon atoms is attached to two identical groups, the compound will not show cis-trans isomerism.

Let us analyze the given options:

(A) $\mathrm{CH_3-CH(CH_3)-CH=CH-CH_2-CH_3}$ : The double-bonded carbons are attached to ($\mathrm{H}$, $\mathrm{-CH(CH_3)_2}$) and ($\mathrm{H}$, $\mathrm{-CH_2CH_3}$). Since both carbons have two different groups, it exhibits cis-trans isomerism.

(B) $\mathrm{CH_3-C(CH_3)=CH-CH_2-CH_3}$ : The left double-bonded carbon is attached to two identical methyl ($\mathrm{-CH_3}$) groups. Therefore, it does NOT exhibit cis-trans isomerism.

(C) $\mathrm{CH_3-CH_2-CH=CH-CH_2-CH_3}$ : The double-bonded carbons are attached to ($\mathrm{H}$, $\mathrm{-CH_2CH_3}$) and ($\mathrm{H}$, $\mathrm{-CH_2CH_3}$). It exhibits cis-trans isomerism.

(D) $\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3}$ : The double-bonded carbons are attached to ($\mathrm{H}$, $\mathrm{-CH_3}$) and ($\mathrm{H}$, $\mathrm{-CH_3}$). It exhibits cis-trans isomerism.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds does not exhibit restricted rotation about its carbon-carbon bonds?
questions.solution:
Restricted rotation about carbon-carbon bonds occurs in molecules with double bonds or ring structures.

Option (A) contains a carbon-carbon double bond ($C=C$), which restricts rotation due to the presence of a $\pi$-bond.

Options (B) and (C) are cyclic compounds (cyclobutane and cyclohexane derivatives). Rotation about the $C-C$ single bonds within a ring is restricted because free rotation would require breaking the ring.

Option (D) is 2,3-dibromobutane, which is an open-chain (acyclic) alkane containing only carbon-carbon single bonds. It allows free rotation about its $C-C$ bonds, leading to various conformational isomers.

Therefore, the compound in option (D) does not exhibit restricted rotation.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following compounds:

(I)      (II)      (III)

Which of the following represents the correct decreasing order of their percentage enol content?
questions.solution:
In compound (I), the carbonyl group is located at the bridge of a bicyclic system (bicyclo[2.2.1]heptan-7-one). The $\alpha$-carbons are bridgehead carbons. According to Bredt's rule, a double bond cannot be formed at a bridgehead position in small bicyclic systems due to high ring strain. Therefore, compound (I) cannot undergo tautomerization, and its enol content is effectively zero.

In compound (II), the carbonyl group is on the ring (bicyclo[2.2.1]heptan-2-one). While enolization cannot occur towards the bridgehead carbon, it can occur towards the adjacent methylene ($\text{-CH}_2\text{-}$) group. Thus, it forms an enol, but without any special stabilization, the enol content remains very low.

In compound (III), 2-nitrocyclohexanone, enolization occurs to form a double bond between the carbons bearing the hydroxyl and nitro groups. This enol form is highly stabilized by extended conjugation with the strongly electron-withdrawing $\text{-NO}_2$ group and by strong intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the enolic $\text{-OH}$ and the oxygen of the $\text{-NO}_2$ group. Consequently, compound (III) has a significantly high enol content.

Therefore, the correct decreasing order of percentage enol content is (III) > (II) > (I).

Answer: (III) > (II) > (I)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds represents an ($E$)-isomer?
questions.solution:
According to the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog (CIP) priority rules, priorities are assigned to the groups attached to each carbon atom of the double bond based on atomic number.

For the compound in Option (C):
On the left carbon atom, the $-\text{CH}_3$ group has a higher priority than the $-\text{H}$ atom (since C > H).
On the right carbon atom, the $-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2$ (isopropyl) group has a higher priority than the $-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$ (ethyl) group. This is because the first carbon of the isopropyl group is bonded to (C, C, H), whereas the first carbon of the ethyl group is bonded to (C, H, H).
The higher priority groups, $-\text{CH}_3$ and $-\text{CH}(\text{CH}_3)_2$, are on opposite sides of the double bond. Thus, it represents the ($E$)-isomer.

For the compound in Option (A):
The higher priority groups ($-\text{CH}_3$ on both sides) are on the same side of the double bond, making it a ($Z$)-isomer.

For the compound in Option (D):
On the left carbon, $-\text{Cl}$ has higher priority than $-\text{F}$. On the right carbon, $-\text{Br}$ has higher priority than $-\text{H}$. Both higher priority groups ($-\text{Cl}$ and $-\text{Br}$) are on the same side, making it a ($Z$)-isomer.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the following compounds has a higher enol content?

(i)

(ii)
questions.solution:
The enolization of the given cyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-one derivatives involves the migration of the hydrogen atom from the C-4 position to the carbonyl oxygen, resulting in the formation of an aromatic phenol ring.

For compound (i), the enol form is $p$-nitrophenol. In this structure, the $-\text{OH}$ group exerts a $+M$ (electron-donating) effect, while the $-\text{NO}_2$ group exerts a $-M$ (electron-withdrawing) effect. This creates a "push-pull" system of extended conjugation, which provides exceptional resonance stabilization to the aromatic ring.

For compound (ii), the enol form is $p$-methoxyphenol. Here, both the $-\text{OH}$ and $-\text{OCH}_3$ groups exert $+M$ effects. The competing electron donation from both groups leads to cross-conjugation and electron repulsion, resulting in significantly less resonance stabilization compared to $p$-nitrophenol.

Additionally, in the keto form of compound (i), the strong $-I$ effect of the $-\text{NO}_2$ group makes the hydrogen at the C-4 position highly acidic, which thermodynamically and kinetically favors the shift towards the enol form.

Due to the greater stability of its aromatic enol form, compound (i) has a higher enol content.

Answer: Compound (i)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following equilibria does not represent a tautomeric relationship?
questions.solution:
Tautomerism involves the migration of an atom (usually hydrogen) from one polyvalent atom to another within the same molecule, accompanied by the rearrangement of single and double bonds.

In option (A), a hydrogen atom migrates from nitrogen to oxygen, representing amide-imidol tautomerism.

In option (B), a hydrogen atom migrates from one nitrogen atom to another, representing triazene tautomerism.

In option (C), the positions of all atoms remain unchanged; only the $\pi$-electrons and lone pairs are delocalized. This represents resonance structures of acrolein, not tautomerism.

In option (D), a hydrogen atom migrates from carbon to oxygen, representing nitroso-oxime tautomerism.

Thus, the relationship in option (C) is resonance, not tautomerism.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Stereoisomers are defined as compounds that have the same molecular and structural formulas, but differ in their:
questions.solution:
Stereoisomers are molecules that have the same molecular formula and sequence of bonded atoms (constitution or structural formula), but differ in the three-dimensional orientations of their atoms in space.

This spatial arrangement of atoms is known as the configuration of the molecule.

Therefore, stereoisomers differ in their configuration.

Answer: configuration

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the given reaction, a mixture of two carbonyl compounds, $X$ and $Y$, reacts with excess phenylhydrazine to yield exactly two organic products, $P$ and $Q$ (counting all stereoisomers).


Which of the following pairs represents the compounds $X$ and $Y$?
questions.solution:
The reaction of a carbonyl compound with phenylhydrazine ($ ext{Ph-NHNH}_2$) yields a phenylhydrazone.

The number of stereoisomers formed depends on the groups attached to the carbonyl carbon:
If the two groups are different ($R_1 eq R_2$), the resulting $ ext{C=N}$ double bond can exhibit geometrical isomerism ($E/Z$), yielding 2 stereoisomers.
If the two groups are identical ($R_1 = R_2$), geometrical isomerism is not possible, yielding only 1 product.

Let us evaluate the given options:

(A) Acetophenone ($ ext{Ph} eq ext{CH}_3$) gives 2 isomers, and acetaldehyde ($ ext{CH}_3 eq ext{H}$) gives 2 isomers. Total = 4 products.

(B) Butanone ($ ext{CH}_3 ext{CH}_2 eq ext{CH}_3$) gives 2 isomers, and acetophenone ($ ext{Ph} eq ext{CH}_3$) gives 2 isomers. Total = 4 products.

(C) Formaldehyde ($ ext{H} = ext{H}$) gives 1 product, and acetaldehyde ($ ext{CH}_3 eq ext{H}$) gives 2 isomers. Total = 3 products.

(D) Formaldehyde ($ ext{H} = ext{H}$) gives 1 product, and acetone ($ ext{CH}_3 = ext{CH}_3$) gives 1 product. Total = 2 products.

Therefore, the mixture of formaldehyde and acetone yields exactly two organic products (one from each), satisfying the condition.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which of the two compounds shown below has the lower enol content?

(i)      (ii)
questions.solution:
The enol content of a compound depends on the relative stability of its enol tautomer.

For compound (i), tautomerization involving the $\alpha$-hydrogens yields 2-hydroxypyrrole. The resulting enol form is a planar cyclic system with complete conjugation and $6\pi$ electrons ($4$ from the two double bonds and $2$ from the nitrogen lone pair). This makes the enol form of compound (i) aromatic, providing it with significant extra stability and leading to a relatively high enol content.

For compound (ii), tautomerization yields a cyclic enol that is non-aromatic. Without any aromatic stabilization, the equilibrium heavily favors the more stable keto (lactam) form due to strong amide resonance.

Since the enol form of compound (i) is stabilized by aromaticity while that of compound (ii) is not, compound (ii) has the lower enol content.

Answer: Compound (ii)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds exhibits geometrical isomerism?
questions.solution:
For an alkene to exhibit geometrical isomerism, each carbon atom of the double bond must be bonded to two different atoms or groups.

In option (A), the left carbon atom is bonded to two identical hydrogen atoms.

In option (B), the left carbon atom is bonded to two identical methyl ($\text{CH}_3$) groups.

In option (C), the right carbon atom is bonded to two identical chlorine atoms.

In option (D), the left carbon atom is bonded to a hydrogen atom and a methyl group, while the right carbon atom is bonded to an iodine atom and a bromine atom. Since both carbon atoms of the double bond are attached to two different groups, this compound exhibits geometrical isomerism.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the number of chiral carbon atoms in the compounds X, Y, and Z, respectively.

X:     Y:     Z:
questions.solution:
To determine the number of chiral carbon atoms, we need to identify the carbon atoms in each compound that are bonded to four different groups.

Compound X:
The structure is 4-methylcyclopentene. The carbon atom bonded to the methyl group (C4) is attached to:
1. A methyl group ($-CH_3$)
2. A hydrogen atom ($-H$)
3. A path around the ring clockwise: $-CH_2-CH=CH-CH_2-$
4. A path around the ring counterclockwise: $-CH_2-CH=CH-CH_2-$
Since the two paths around the ring are identical, C4 is attached to two identical groups. Therefore, it is not a chiral center. The molecule has a plane of symmetry passing through C4 and bisecting the double bond.
Number of chiral carbons in X = $0$.

Compound Y:
The structure is 1,3-dimethylcyclohexane. The carbon atoms bonded to the methyl groups (C1 and C3) are each attached to:
1. A methyl group ($-CH_3$)
2. A hydrogen atom ($-H$)
3. A path around the ring: $-CH_2-CH(CH_3)-$
4. The other path around the ring: $-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-$
Since the two paths are different, both C1 and C3 are bonded to four different groups. Thus, both are chiral centers.
Number of chiral carbons in Y = $2$.

Compound Z:
The structure is 3-methylcyclohexene. The carbon atom bonded to the methyl group (C3) is attached to:
1. A methyl group ($-CH_3$)
2. A hydrogen atom ($-H$)
3. A path around the ring starting with the double bond: $-CH=CH-CH_2-CH_2-$
4. A path around the ring starting with single bonds: $-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH=$
Since the two paths are different, C3 is bonded to four different groups and is a chiral center.
Number of chiral carbons in Z = $1$.

Therefore, the number of chiral carbon atoms in compounds X, Y, and Z are $0$, $2$, and $1$, respectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the number of chiral carbon atoms present in the following compound:
questions.solution:
A chiral carbon atom is one that is bonded to four different atoms or groups.

Let us analyze the given structure by examining each carbon atom:

The carbon atom bonded to the $\text{-OH}$ group is attached to four different groups: $\text{-H}$, $\text{-OH}$, $\text{-CH}_3$, and $\text{-CH}_2\text{CH(Br)CH(C}_2\text{H}_5\text{)CH}_3$. Thus, it is a chiral carbon.

The carbon atom bonded to the $\text{-Br}$ atom is attached to four different groups: $\text{-H}$, $\text{-Br}$, $\text{-CH}_2\text{CH(OH)CH}_3$, and $\text{-CH(C}_2\text{H}_5\text{)CH}_3$. Thus, it is a chiral carbon.

The carbon atom bonded to the $\text{-C}_2\text{H}_5$ group is attached to four different groups: $\text{-H}$, $\text{-CH}_3$, $\text{-C}_2\text{H}_5$, and $\text{-CH(Br)CH}_2\text{CH(OH)CH}_3$. Thus, it is a chiral carbon.

The remaining carbon atoms are bonded to at least two identical hydrogen atoms, so they are not chiral.

Therefore, the compound contains $3$ chiral carbon atoms.

Answer: 3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the total number of geometrical isomers possible for the following compound.
questions.solution:
To find the total number of geometrical isomers, we first need to identify the number of stereogenic centers (units capable of showing geometrical isomerism) in the given molecule.

The given compound is $\text{Ph}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$.
Let's analyze the stereogenic units:
1. The double bond on the left ($\text{Ph}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-$): The carbon atoms of this double bond are attached to different groups ($\text{H}$, $\text{Ph}$ and $\text{H}$, cyclohexyl). Thus, it can show cis-trans (or E/Z) isomerism.
2. The double bond on the right ($-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$): The carbon atoms of this double bond are also attached to different groups ($\text{H}$, cyclohexyl and $\text{H}$, $\text{CH}_3$). Thus, it can also show cis-trans (or E/Z) isomerism.
3. The cyclohexane ring: It is a 1,4-disubstituted cyclohexane ring. Since it has two substituents at different positions, it can exist in cis and trans forms.

Therefore, the total number of stereogenic units $n = 3$.

Next, we check for symmetry in the molecule. The two terminal groups are different (a phenyl group on one side and a methyl group on the other). Because the ends are different, the molecule is unsymmetrical.

For an unsymmetrical molecule with $n$ stereogenic units, the total number of geometrical isomers is given by the formula $2^n$.

Total geometrical isomers $= 2^3 = 8$.

Answer: 8

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of geometrical isomers possible for the compound $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}$ is:
questions.solution:
The given compound is an unsymmetrical diene: $\mathrm{CH}_{3}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}-\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}$.

The two ends of the carbon chain are different ($\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{C}_{2}\mathrm{H}_{5}$).

For an unsymmetrical polyene with $n$ double bonds capable of showing geometrical isomerism, the number of geometrical isomers is given by $2^n$.

Here, the number of double bonds is $n = 2$.

Number of geometrical isomers = $2^2 = 4$.

The four isomers are (cis, cis), (cis, trans), (trans, cis), and (trans, trans).

Answer: 4

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the number of cis-trans isomers possible for the given compound.
questions.solution:
Let us analyze the given structure to find the number of stereocenters capable of showing geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism.

The given compound has the structure: Cyclopentylidene-CH=CH-C$\equiv$CH.
There are two double bonds in this molecule:
1. An exocyclic double bond connecting the cyclopentane ring to the chain.
2. An internal double bond in the carbon chain (-CH=CH-).

Now, we check each double bond for geometrical isomerism:

1. Exocyclic double bond: For a double bond to show geometrical isomerism, each of the two double-bonded carbon atoms must be attached to two different groups. The carbon atom of the exocyclic double bond that is part of the ring is attached to two identical paths around the unsubstituted cyclopentane ring (both paths are -CH_2-CH_2-). Because these two "groups" are identical, this double bond cannot show cis-trans isomerism.

2. Internal double bond (-CH=CH-): The carbon atoms of this double bond are each attached to a hydrogen atom and a larger group. One carbon is attached to H and a -CH=Cyclopentylidene group, while the other is attached to H and a -C\equiv CH group. Since both carbons are attached to two different groups, this double bond can exist in *cis* and *trans* configurations.

The triple bond (-C\equiv CH) is linear and does not show geometrical isomerism.

Since there is only $1$ double bond capable of showing geometrical isomerism, the total number of *cis*-*trans* isomers is $2^n = 2^1 = 2$.

Answer: 2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds represents the $Z$-isomer?
questions.solution:
To determine whether an isomer is $E$ or $Z$, we assign priorities to the groups attached to each carbon atom of the double bond using the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog (CIP) rules. The group with the higher atomic number of the directly attached atom gets higher priority. If the two highest-priority groups are on the same side of the double bond, it is the $Z$-isomer; if they are on opposite sides, it is the $E$-isomer.

Let us analyze each option:

Option (A):
Left carbon: The groups are $-\text{COOH}$ and $-\text{H}$. Since $\text{C}$ has a higher atomic number than $\text{H}$, $-\text{COOH}$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{H}$ gets priority 2.
Right carbon: The groups are $-\text{COOH}$ and $-\text{H}$. Similarly, $-\text{COOH}$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{H}$ gets priority 2.
Both high-priority groups ($-\text{COOH}$) are on the same side (top) of the double bond. Therefore, this is the $Z$-isomer.

Option (B):
Left carbon: The groups are $-\text{CH}_3$ and $-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$. $-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{CH}_3$ gets priority 2.
Right carbon: The groups are $-\text{Cl}$ and $-\text{COOH}$. Since $\text{Cl}$ (atomic number 17) > $\text{C}$ (atomic number 6), $-\text{Cl}$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{COOH}$ gets priority 2.
The high-priority groups ($-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$ at the bottom and $-\text{Cl}$ at the top) are on opposite sides. Therefore, this is the $E$-isomer.

Option (C):
Left carbon: The groups are $-\text{Cl}$ and $-\text{F}$. Since $\text{Cl}$ (17) > $\text{F}$ (9), $-\text{Cl}$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{F}$ gets priority 2.
Right carbon: The groups are $-\text{H}$ and $-\text{Br}$. Since $\text{Br}$ (35) > $\text{H}$ (1), $-\text{Br}$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{H}$ gets priority 2.
The high-priority groups ($-\text{Cl}$ at the top and $-\text{Br}$ at the bottom) are on opposite sides. Therefore, this is the $E$-isomer.

Option (D):
Left carbon: The groups are $-\text{Cl}$ and $-\text{F}$. $-\text{Cl}$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{F}$ gets priority 2.
Right carbon: The groups are $-\text{CH}_3$ and $-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$. $-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$ gets priority 1 and $-\text{CH}_3$ gets priority 2.
The high-priority groups ($-\text{Cl}$ at the top and $-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$ at the bottom) are on opposite sides. Therefore, this is the $E$-isomer.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Meso-tartaric acid and d-tartaric acid are related to each other as:
questions.solution:
Meso-tartaric acid and d-tartaric acid are stereoisomers of each other.

Meso-tartaric acid is an optically inactive stereoisomer due to the presence of an internal plane of symmetry, whereas d-tartaric acid is an optically active stereoisomer.

Since they are stereoisomers but not mirror images of each other, they are classified as diastereomers.

Answer: diastereomers

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism?
questions.solution:
For a cyclic compound to exhibit geometrical isomerism, at least two carbon atoms in the ring must each be bonded to two different substituents.

In option (A), 1,2,3-trimethylcyclopropane, three carbon atoms are each bonded to a hydrogen atom and a methyl group (two different substituents). Thus, it can exhibit geometrical isomerism.

In option (B), 1,1-dibromo-3,3-dichlorocyclobutane, the substituted carbon atoms are bonded to identical groups (two bromine atoms on one carbon, and two chlorine atoms on the other). Since no two carbons have two different substituents, swapping any groups does not create a new spatial arrangement. Therefore, it cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism.

In option (C), 1,2,3,4-tetramethylcyclobutane, four carbon atoms are each bonded to a hydrogen atom and a methyl group. Thus, it can exhibit geometrical isomerism.

In option (D), 1,2-dimethylcyclohexane, two carbon atoms are each bonded to a hydrogen atom and a methyl group. Thus, it can exhibit geometrical isomerism (cis and trans forms).

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the total number of geometrical isomers possible for the following compound:
questions.solution:
The given compound is 1,4-dicyclopropylbuta-1,3-diene. Its structure can be represented as $R-CH=CH-CH=CH-R$, where $R$ is a cyclopropyl group.

The molecule is symmetrical because both ends of the diene chain are attached to identical cyclopropyl groups. The number of double bonds capable of showing geometrical isomerism is $n = 2$.

For a symmetrical molecule with an even number of double bonds ($n$), the total number of geometrical isomers is given by the formula:
Number of geometrical isomers = $2^{n-1} + 2^{(n/2)-1}$

Substituting $n = 2$ into the formula:
Number of geometrical isomers = $2^{2-1} + 2^{(2/2)-1}$
Number of geometrical isomers = $2^1 + 2^0$
Number of geometrical isomers = $2 + 1 = 3$

The three geometrical isomers are:
1. (E, E) or trans, trans
2. (Z, Z) or cis, cis
3. (E, Z) or trans, cis (which is identical to (Z, E) due to the symmetry of the molecule).

Therefore, the total number of geometrical isomers possible is 3.

Answer: 3

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the total number of optically active isomers corresponding to the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{4}\mathrm{H}_{9}\mathrm{Br}$?
questions.solution:
The structural isomers for the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_{4}\mathrm{H}_{9}\mathrm{Br}$ are:

1. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Br}$ (1-Bromobutane) - Achiral

2. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{Br})\mathrm{CH}_{3}$ (2-Bromobutane) - Chiral

3. $\mathrm{CH}_{3}\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{CH}_{3})\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{Br}$ (1-Bromo-2-methylpropane) - Achiral

4. $(\mathrm{CH}_{3})_{3}\mathrm{CBr}$ (2-Bromo-2-methylpropane) - Achiral

Only 2-bromobutane contains a chiral carbon atom, as it is bonded to four different groups ($-\mathrm{H}$, $-\mathrm{Br}$, $-\mathrm{CH}_{3}$, and $-\mathrm{CH}_{2}\mathrm{CH}_{3}$). Therefore, it exists as a pair of enantiomers (dextro and laevo rotatory).

Thus, the total number of optically active isomers is $2$.

Answer: 2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following three structures:

(I)

(II)

(III)

Which of the following statements is correct regarding their chirality?
questions.solution:
For a molecule to be chiral, it must lack a plane of symmetry, center of symmetry, and alternating axis of symmetry. The presence of a chiral center (a carbon atom bonded to four different groups) usually indicates chirality in molecules without multiple chiral centers.

In structure (I), $C_2H_5-CH(CH_3)-C_2H_5$, the central carbon atom is bonded to a hydrogen atom, a methyl group, and two identical ethyl ($-C_2H_5$) groups. Since two groups are identical, it is not a chiral center. The molecule has a plane of symmetry and is achiral.

In structure (II), $(CH_3)_2CH-CH(OH)-CH(CH_3)_2$, the central carbon atom is bonded to a hydrogen atom, a hydroxyl group, and two identical isopropyl ($-CH(CH_3)_2$) groups. Since two groups are identical, it is not a chiral center. The molecule has a plane of symmetry and is achiral.

In structure (III), $CH_3-CH(OH)-CH(CH_3)_2$, the carbon atom bonded to the $-OH$ group is attached to four different groups: a hydrogen atom, a hydroxyl ($-OH$) group, a methyl ($-CH_3$) group, and an isopropyl ($-CH(CH_3)_2$) group. Because all four groups are different, this carbon is a chiral center. The molecule lacks any element of symmetry and is chiral.

Therefore, only structure (III) is chiral.

Answer: Only (III) is chiral.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following characteristics is common to both cis-2-butene and trans-2-butene?
questions.solution:
Hydrogenation of both cis-2-butene and trans-2-butene yields the same product, which is n-butane.

Heat of hydrogenation is different because trans-2-butene is more stable than cis-2-butene, resulting in a lower heat of hydrogenation for the trans isomer.

Boiling point is different because cis-2-butene has a net dipole moment, leading to stronger intermolecular dipole-dipole interactions and a higher boiling point compared to trans-2-butene.

Dipole moment is different because trans-2-butene is symmetrical and has a zero dipole moment, whereas cis-2-butene has a non-zero dipole moment.

Answer: Product of hydrogenation

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds contains an asymmetric carbon atom?
questions.solution:
An asymmetric (chiral) carbon atom is one that is bonded to four different atoms or groups of atoms.

Let us examine the carbon atoms in each given compound:

(A) In $CH_3-CH_2Cl$, the left carbon is bonded to three identical hydrogen atoms, and the right carbon is bonded to two identical hydrogen atoms. Thus, it has no asymmetric carbon.

(B) In $BrCH_2-CH(OH)CH_3$, the right carbon atom is attached to four different groups: $-H$, $-OH$, $-CH_3$, and $-CH_2Br$. Therefore, it is an asymmetric carbon atom.

(C) In $CH_3-CHCl_2$, the left carbon is bonded to three identical hydrogen atoms, and the right carbon is bonded to two identical chlorine atoms. Thus, it has no asymmetric carbon.

(D) In $ClCH_2-CH_2Br$, both carbon atoms are bonded to two identical hydrogen atoms. Thus, it has no asymmetric carbon.

Hence, the compound in option (B) contains an asymmetric carbon atom.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the absolute configurations ($R$ or $S$) of the following compounds respectively:

Compound I:
Compound II:
Compound III:
questions.solution:
Applying Cahn-Ingold-Prelog (CIP) priority rules to Compound I, the priorities are $-OH$ (1) > $-CF_{3}$ (2) > $-Ph$ (3) > $-H$ (4).

The lowest priority group ($-H$) is on a dashed bond, pointing away from the viewer. The sequence of groups 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 traces a clockwise direction, giving the absolute configuration $R$.

In Compound II, the priorities are $-NH_{2}$ (1) > $-CH_{2}SH$ (2) > $-COOH$ (3) > $-H$ (4). For $-CH_{2}SH$, the first attached carbon is bonded to (S, H, H), while for $-COOH$, it is bonded to (O, O, O). Since the atomic number of S (16) is greater than O (8), $-CH_{2}SH$ receives higher priority.

The lowest priority group ($-H$) in Compound II is on a solid wedged bond, pointing towards the viewer. The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 traces a counter-clockwise direction. Due to the wedged $-H$, the apparent $S$ configuration is reversed, yielding an absolute configuration of $R$.

In Compound III, the priorities are $-CH_{2}CHO$ (1) > $-CH_{2}CH=C(CH_{3})_{2}$ (2) > $-CH_{3}$ (3) > $-H$ (4). The carbon in $-CH_{2}CHO$ is attached to a carbonyl carbon (treated as bonded to O, O, H), whereas in $-CH_{2}CH=C(CH_{3})_{2}$ it is attached to an alkene carbon (treated as bonded to C, C, H). Since O > C, $-CH_{2}CHO$ has higher priority.

The lowest priority group ($-H$) in Compound III is on a dashed bond. The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 traces a clockwise direction. Thus, the absolute configuration is $R$.

The absolute configurations of the three compounds respectively are $R$, $R$, $R$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds has the $S$ configuration?
questions.solution:
According to the Cahn-Ingold-Prelog priority rules, the priority of the groups attached to the chiral center is determined by atomic number.

The priorities are: $-\text{Br} (1) > -\text{OH} (2) > -\text{CH}_3 (3) > -\text{H} (4)$.

In a Fischer projection, if the lowest priority group is on a vertical bond, the actual configuration is the same as the apparent configuration ($R$ for clockwise, $S$ for counter-clockwise). If it is on a horizontal bond, the actual configuration is the reverse of the apparent one.

In option (A), $-\text{H}$ is on the vertical bond. The sequence $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$ goes from right to left to top, which is clockwise. The configuration is $R$.

In option (B), $-\text{H}$ is on the horizontal bond. The sequence $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$ goes from bottom to right to top, which is counter-clockwise. Reversing this gives the $R$ configuration.

In option (C), $-\text{H}$ is on the vertical bond. The sequence $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$ goes from left to top to right, which is clockwise. The configuration is $R$.

In option (D), $-\text{H}$ is on the vertical bond. The sequence $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3$ goes from left to right to top, which is counter-clockwise. The configuration is $S$.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Determine the absolute configurations of the chiral centres in compounds (i) and (ii).

(i)     (ii)
questions.solution:
Assign priorities to the four groups attached to the chiral center using Cahn-Ingold-Prelog (CIP) rules.

The atoms directly attached to the chiral center are C, C, C, and H. Hydrogen has the lowest priority (4).

For the remaining groups (-COOCH3, -COOH, and -CH3):
In -COOCH3, the carbon is bonded to (O, O, O) and the oxygen atoms are further bonded to (C, C, C) considering phantom atoms for the double bond.
In -COOH, the carbon is bonded to (O, O, O) and the oxygen atoms are further bonded to (C, C, H).
In -CH3, the carbon is bonded to (H, H, H).

Comparing -COOCH3 and -COOH, the first point of difference is C versus H. Thus, -COOCH3 has a higher priority than -COOH.

The priorities are: 1: -COOCH3, 2: -COOH, 3: -CH3, 4: -H.

For compound (i):
In the Fischer projection, the lowest priority group (-H) is on a horizontal bond.
The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 (-COOCH3 $\rightarrow$ -COOH $\rightarrow$ -CH3) goes from bottom to top to right, which is a clockwise direction.
Since group 4 is on a horizontal bond, the actual configuration is opposite to the apparent one. The apparent configuration is R, so the actual configuration is S.

For compound (ii):
In the 3D representation, the lowest priority group (-H) is on a dashed bond, pointing away from the viewer.
The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 (-COOCH3 $\rightarrow$ -COOH $\rightarrow$ -CH3) goes from right to down-left to up, which is a clockwise direction.
Since group 4 is pointing away, the configuration is R.

The absolute configurations are S and R respectively.

Answer: $S, R$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds has a plane of symmetry (POS) but not a centre of symmetry (COS)?
questions.solution:
Evaluating each compound for the presence of a plane of symmetry (POS) and a centre of symmetry (COS).

Compound (A): A vertical diagonal plane passing through the bottom-left and top-right carbon atoms acts as a POS. The top-left carbon (Br up, H down) reflects perfectly onto the bottom-right carbon (Br up, H down). However, it lacks a COS. For a COS to exist, the top-left Br (up) must invert to a Br (down) at the diametrically opposite bottom-right position, but it is Br (up). Thus, (A) has a POS but no COS.

Compound (B): 1,4-difluorobenzene is a planar molecule. It possesses multiple planes of symmetry (including the molecular plane) and a centre of symmetry at the center of the benzene ring.

Compound (C): In trans-1,3-dimethylcyclobutane, a vertical plane passing through the left and right carbons acts as a POS, reflecting the top CH2 group onto the bottom CH2 group. It also has a COS at the center of the ring, as the left carbon's substituents (H up, CH3 down) invert to match the right carbon's substituents (H down, CH3 up).

Compound (D): Due to the asymmetric placement of different halogens (F, Cl, Br), this molecule lacks both a POS and a COS.

Therefore, only compound (A) has a POS but not a COS.

Answer:

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following instruments is used to measure the optical activity (specific rotation) of a substance?
questions.solution:
Optical activity is the property of a chiral substance to rotate the plane of plane-polarized light.

The instrument used to measure the angle of rotation (specific rotation) caused by an optically active substance is a polarimeter.

A voltmeter measures electrical potential difference, a photometer measures light intensity, and a refractometer measures the refractive index of a substance.

Answer: Polarimeter

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Identify the relationship between the two compounds given below:
questions.solution:
Let C1 be the chiral carbon bonded to -Br, -D, and -H.
Let C2 be the chiral carbon bonded to -Cl, -I, and -H.

For the first compound:
C1 is the top carbon. The CIP priorities are -Br (1) > -C2 (2) > -D (3) > -H (4). The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 is clockwise. Since the lowest priority group (-H) is on a horizontal bond, the configuration is S.
C2 is the bottom carbon. The CIP priorities are -I (1) > -Cl (2) > -C1 (3) > -H (4). The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 is counter-clockwise. Since -H is on a horizontal bond, the configuration is R.
The configuration of the first compound is (1S, 2R).

For the second compound:
C2 is the top carbon. The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 (I $\rightarrow$ Cl $\rightarrow$ C1) is clockwise. Since -H is on a horizontal bond, the configuration is S.
C1 is the bottom carbon. The sequence 1 $\rightarrow$ 2 $\rightarrow$ 3 (Br $\rightarrow$ C2 $\rightarrow$ D) is counter-clockwise. Since -H is on a horizontal bond, the configuration is R.
The configuration of the second compound is (1R, 2S).

Since the configurations at all chiral centers are exactly opposite (1S $\rightarrow$ 1R and 2R $\rightarrow$ 2S), the two compounds are enantiomers.

Alternatively, rotating the second Fischer projection by $180^\circ$ in the plane gives a top carbon with Br (top), H (left), D (right) and a bottom carbon with Cl (bottom), H (left), I (right). Comparing this to the first structure, both chiral centers differ by exactly one swap of groups (D/Br on top, I/Cl on bottom), confirming they are enantiomers.

Answer: Enantiomers

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many optically active stereoisomers exist for 2,3-dichlorobutane?
questions.solution:
The structure of 2,3-dichlorobutane is $CH_3-CH(Cl)-CH(Cl)-CH_3$.

It contains two similar chiral carbon atoms ($n=2$).

Since the molecule is symmetrical and has an even number of chiral centers, the number of optically active stereoisomers is given by $2^{n-1}$.

Substituting $n=2$, the number of optically active isomers is $2^{2-1} = 2$.

The number of meso isomers is $2^{\dfrac{n}{2}-1} = 2^{1-1} = 2^0 = 1$.

The two optically active isomers are the $(2R, 3R)$ and $(2S, 3S)$ enantiomers, which form a racemic mixture.

The meso isomer is $(2R, 3S)$, which is optically inactive due to the presence of an internal plane of symmetry.

Therefore, there are 2 optically active stereoisomers.

Answer: 2

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many stereoisomers can exist for the following acid?
questions.solution:
The given molecule is 2,4-dihydroxy-3-carboxy-pentanedioic acid. Its structure can be written as:

$\text{HOOC}-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-\text{CH}(\text{COOH})-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-\text{COOH}$

Let us identify the stereocenters in the molecule. The carbons C-2 and C-4 are chiral centers because they are attached to four different groups. The central carbon C-3 is attached to a hydrogen atom, a carboxyl group, and two $-\text{CH}(\text{OH})\text{COOH}$ groups.

When C-2 and C-4 have opposite configurations (one is $R$ and the other is $S$), the two $-\text{CH}(\text{OH})\text{COOH}$ groups become non-identical (enantiomeric), making C-3 a pseudo-chiral center. When C-2 and C-4 have the same configuration ($R,R$ or $S,S$), the two groups are identical, and C-3 is not a chiral center.

The molecule is symmetrical (it can be divided into two equal halves). For a symmetrical molecule with an odd number of stereocenters ($n = 3$):

Number of optically active isomers $= 2^{n-1} - 2^{(n-1)/2}$
$\Rightarrow 2^{3-1} - 2^{(3-1)/2} = 4 - 2 = 2$

Number of meso isomers $= 2^{(n-1)/2}$
$\Rightarrow 2^{(3-1)/2} = 2^1 = 2$

Total number of stereoisomers $=$ Optically active isomers $+$ Meso isomers
$\Rightarrow 2 + 2 = 4$

The four stereoisomers are:
1. $(2R, 4R)$ - optically active
2. $(2S, 4S)$ - optically active
3. $(2R, 3r, 4S)$ - meso
4. $(2R, 3s, 4S)$ - meso

Answer: 4

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A mixture contains $6\text{ g}$ of $(+)$-2-butanol and $4\text{ g}$ of $(-)$-2-butanol. If the specific rotation of enantiomerically pure $(+)$-2-butanol is $+13.5$ units, what are the enantiomeric excess and the observed specific rotation of the mixture, respectively?
questions.solution:
Mass of $(+)$-2-butanol $= 6\text{ g}$

Mass of $(-)$-2-butanol $= 4\text{ g}$

Total mass $= 6 + 4 = 10\text{ g}$

Enantiomeric excess $(ee)$ is given by:

$ee = \dfrac{|m_+ - m_-|}{m_+ + m_-} \times 100\%$

$ee = \dfrac{6 - 4}{10} \times 100\% = 20\%$

The observed specific rotation is related to the enantiomeric excess by:

Observed specific rotation $= \dfrac{ee}{100} \times [\alpha]_{pure}$

Observed specific rotation $= \dfrac{20}{100} \times (+13.5) = +2.7\text{ units}$

Answer: $20\%, +2.7$ unit

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes the possible stereoisomers for the molecule $\mathrm{HOOC-CH=C=CH-COOH}$?
options: Two optical and two geometrical isomers
questions.solution:
The given molecule $\mathrm{HOOC-CH=C=CH-COOH}$ is an allene with an even number of cumulative double bonds.

The central carbon is $sp$ hybridized while the terminal carbons are $sp^2$ hybridized. The two $\pi$ bonds are formed by mutually perpendicular $p$ orbitals, causing the groups attached to the terminal carbons to lie in perpendicular planes.

Since the groups on each terminal carbon are different ($\mathrm{H}$ and $\mathrm{COOH}$), the molecule lacks both a plane of symmetry and a center of symmetry.

Therefore, the molecule is chiral and exists as a pair of enantiomers (two optical isomers).

Because the terminal groups are in perpendicular planes, the molecule cannot exhibit geometrical isomerism.

Answer: Two optical isomers

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the total number of stereoisomers for the following compound:
questions.solution:
The given compound is $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH(Cl)}-\text{CH(Br)}-\text{CH(OH)}-\text{CH}_3$.

Let us identify the chiral centers in the molecule:
1. C-2 is bonded to four different groups: $-\text{CH}_3$, $-\text{H}$, $-\text{Cl}$, and $-\text{CH(Br)CH(OH)CH}_3$. Thus, it is a chiral center.
2. C-3 is bonded to four different groups: $-\text{CH(Cl)CH}_3$, $-\text{H}$, $-\text{Br}$, and $-\text{CH(OH)CH}_3$. Thus, it is a chiral center.
3. C-4 is bonded to four different groups: $-\text{CH(Br)CH(Cl)CH}_3$, $-\text{H}$, $-\text{OH}$, and $-\text{CH}_3$. Thus, it is a chiral center.

The number of chiral centers ($n$) is $3$.

Since the two ends of the carbon chain are different (one has a $-\text{Cl}$ group and the other has an $-\text{OH}$ group), the molecule is unsymmetrical. It cannot have any plane of symmetry or center of symmetry in any of its configurations.

For an unsymmetrical molecule with $n$ chiral centers, the total number of stereoisomers is given by the formula $2^n$.

Total number of stereoisomers $= 2^3 = 8$.

Answer: 8

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the total number of stereoisomers for the compound shown below:
questions.solution:
The given compound is 2,3,4-tribromopentane, which has the structural formula $CH_3-CH(Br)-CH(Br)-CH(Br)-CH_3$.

It contains $3$ stereocenters at the C2, C3, and C4 positions.

The molecule is symmetrical, meaning it can be divided into two identical halves.

For a symmetrical molecule with an odd number of stereocenters ($n$), the total number of stereoisomers is given by the formula $2^{n-1}$.

Substituting $n = 3$ into the formula:

Total stereoisomers = $2^{3-1} = 2^2 = 4$

These $4$ stereoisomers consist of two optically active enantiomers and two meso compounds.

The optically active enantiomers are $(2R, 4R)$ and $(2S, 4S)$. In these isomers, C3 is not a chiral center because the two groups attached to it are identical.

The meso compounds are $(2R, 3r, 4S)$ and $(2R, 3s, 4S)$. In these isomers, C3 acts as a pseudoasymmetric center because the two groups attached to it are enantiomeric.

Therefore, the total number of stereoisomers is $4$.

Answer: 4

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the total number of stereoisomers for the following compound:
questions.solution:
The given compound is $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}(\text{Cl})-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$.

Let us identify the stereogenic centers in the molecule:
1. The double bond $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-$ can exhibit geometrical isomerism (E/Z).
2. The carbon atom attached to chlorine ($-\text{CH}(\text{Cl})-$) is bonded to four different groups: $-\text{H}$, $-\text{Cl}$, $-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_3$, and $-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$. Thus, it is a chiral center.
3. The double bond $-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$ can also exhibit geometrical isomerism (E/Z).

The total number of stereocenters is $n = 3$.

Since the two terminal groups of the molecule are different ($-\text{CH}_3$ and $-\text{C}_2\text{H}_5$), the molecule is unsymmetrical. It does not possess any plane or center of symmetry.

For an unsymmetrical molecule with $n$ stereocenters, the total number of stereoisomers is given by the formula $2^n$.

Total number of stereoisomers = $2^3 = 8$.

Answer: 8

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Determine the total number of stereoisomers for the compound shown below:
questions.solution:
The given compound is 3-bromobutan-2-ol, with the structure $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-\text{CH}(\text{Br})-\text{CH}_3$.

First, we identify the chiral centers in the molecule. A chiral center is a carbon atom bonded to four different groups.
Carbon-2 is bonded to $-\text{CH}_3$, $-\text{OH}$, $-\text{H}$, and $-\text{CH}(\text{Br})\text{CH}_3$. Since all four groups are different, C-2 is a chiral center.
Carbon-3 is bonded to $-\text{CH}_3$, $-\text{Br}$, $-\text{H}$, and $-\text{CH}(\text{OH})\text{CH}_3$. Since all four groups are different, C-3 is also a chiral center.

The molecule has $n = 2$ chiral centers.

Next, we check for symmetry. The two ends of the molecule are different (one has an $-\text{OH}$ group and the other has a $-\text{Br}$ group). Therefore, the molecule is unsymmetrical and cannot form any meso compounds.

For an unsymmetrical molecule with $n$ chiral centers, the total number of stereoisomers is given by the formula $2^n$.

Substituting $n = 2$:
Total stereoisomers $= 2^2 = 4$

The four stereoisomers are two pairs of enantiomers: (2R, 3R), (2S, 3S) and (2R, 3S), (2S, 3R).

Answer: 4

questions.subject: Chemistry
